title: Python for Reverse Engineering
subtitle: Practical Python reference for parsing, decoding, emulating logic, and rebuilding algorithms from assembly or decompiler output.

sections.title: Scope and Safe Use
sections.text: This sheet is aimed at CTFs, crackmes, malware labs, protocol reversing, file format analysis, and other authorized reverse engineering. The biggest use of Python in RE is not fancy tooling first — it is quickly reconstructing logic from assembly or pseudocode so you can test assumptions, verify inputs, and automate tedious analysis.

sections.title: When Python Helps Most
sections.text: Use Python when a function is easier to re-express than single-step in a debugger. Good examples are: custom checksum routines, XOR/ROL string decoders, key validation logic, table lookups, bitfield parsing, file format unpacking, and hash reproduction. A short script often gives faster answers than prolonged manual tracing.

sections.title: CORE BUILT-INS YOU WILL USE CONSTANTLY

items.value: Pull data from logs / decompiler text
Read a BE field from a buffer
Assembly and decompilers often differ in sign interpretation
Make it correct before making it pretty
Print intermediate values against debugger observations
Especially for shifts, rotates, and xor/add chains
Recover hidden strings/config from a transform
Rebuild checksum or key verification routines
Produce values that satisfy validation logic in a crackme/lab
Search small keyspaces or constrained values
Execute a tiny function model outside the target binary

sections.title: EXAMPLE: SIMPLE XOR DECODER
sections.code:
data = bytes.fromhex('1023324150')
key = 0x55
decoded = bytes(b ^ key for b in data)
print(decoded)
print(decoded.decode('utf-8', errors='ignore'))

sections.title: EXAMPLE: REPEATING-KEY XOR
sections.code:
data = b'\x10\x20\x30\x40\x50'
key = b'KEY'
out = bytes(data[i] ^ key[i % len(key)] for i in range(len(data)))
print(out.hex())

sections.title: EXAMPLE: LITTLE-ENDIAN PARSING
sections.code:
import struct

buf = bytes.fromhex('78563412efbeadde')
a, b = struct.unpack('<II', buf)
print(hex(a), hex(b))

sections.title: EXAMPLE: MANUAL ROL32
sections.code:
def rol32(x, r):
    x &= 0xffffffff
    return ((x << r) | (x >> (32 - r))) & 0xffffffff

print(hex(rol32(0x12345678, 8)))

sections.title: EXAMPLE: REBUILDING A CHECKSUM FROM PSEUDOCODE
sections.code:
def checksum(data: bytes) -> int:
    acc = 0x1337
    for b in data:
        acc = ((acc << 5) + acc) ^ b
        acc &= 0xffffffff
    return acc

print(hex(checksum(b'test')))

sections.title: EXAMPLE: INPUT GENERATOR FROM GHIDRA-LIKE LOGIC
sections.code:
# Suppose decompiler logic was effectively:
# sum(input[i] ^ 0x20 for i in range(4)) == 300

target = 300
candidate = b'ABCD'
value = sum(c ^ 0x20 for c in candidate)
print(candidate, value)

# Replace the candidate generation logic with brute force or constraints as needed.

sections.title: EXAMPLE: SMALL CRACKME / LAB BRUTE FORCE HARNESS
About Crackers / Keygens in Practice
sections.text: For crackmes, CTFs, and authorized labs, the usual Python workflow is: rebuild the check routine, validate your reimplementation with known examples, then either derive the valid input algebraically or brute force the constrained search space. In practice, most scripts are really 'input generators' or 'check reimplementations' rather than anything magical.

sections.title: EXAMPLE: REIMPLEMENTING A KEY CHECK FROM PSEUDOCODE
EXAMPLE: PARSING A CUSTOM RECORD
sections.code:
import struct

buf = bytes.fromhex('0100040041424344')
rtype, length = struct.unpack('<HH', buf[:4])
payload = buf[4:4+length]
print(rtype, length, payload)

items.value: Packing, ELF helpers, networking, remote automation

sections.title: EXAMPLE: CAPSTONE DISASSEMBLY
sections.code:
from capstone import Cs, CS_ARCH_X86, CS_MODE_64

code = bytes.fromhex('4831c0c3')
md = Cs(CS_ARCH_X86, CS_MODE_64)
for insn in md.disasm(code, 0x1000):
    print(hex(insn.address), insn.mnemonic, insn.op_str)

sections.title: EXAMPLE: PWNLIB PACK / UNPACK
sections.code:
from pwn import p32, p64, u32, u64

print(p32(0x12345678))
print(hex(u32(b'ABCD')))

sections.title: GOOD HABITS WHEN WRITING RE SCRIPTS

items.value: Verify against known outputs from debugger / target

items.key: Prefer bytes over str for binary work
Comment where the logic came from
items.value: Function name, address, or pseudocode source
